mission.title: Install and Configure Weaviate
mission.description: Weaviate is an open-source vector database designed for semantic search and structured filtering. It is ideal for applications requiring scalable and fault-tolerant storage of both objects and vectors. Key trade-offs include the need for proper resource allocation and potential complexity in managing vector embeddings.

steps.title: Install the Weaviate CLI
steps.description:
This step installs the Weaviate command-line interface, which is essential for managing Weaviate deployments.

```bash

curl -LO https://github.com/weaviate/weaviate/releases/download/v1.36.2/weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/weaviate

```

> This command downloads the CLI binary, makes it executable, and moves it to a directory in your PATH.
commands: curl -LO https://github.com/weaviate/weaviate/releases/download/v1.36.2/weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/weaviate

steps.title: Create a Weaviate cluster
steps.description:
This command creates a new Weaviate cluster on your managed Kubernetes service with the specified version and node count.

```bash

weaviate create cluster --version 1.36.2 --node-count 3 --region us-central1

```

> Adjust the region and node count as per your requirements.

steps.title: Set up kubeconfig
steps.description:
This step configures your kubeconfig file to interact with the newly created Weaviate cluster.

```bash

gcloud container clusters get-credentials weaviate-cluster --region us-central1

```

> Replace 'weaviate-cluster' with the actual name of your cluster.
commands: gcloud container clusters get-credentials weaviate-cluster --region us-central1

steps.title: Verify the nodes
steps.description:
This command checks the status of the nodes in your Weaviate cluster to ensure they are running properly.

```bash

kubectl get nodes

```

> You should see all nodes in 'Ready' status.

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
This step configures autoscaling and monitoring for your Weaviate deployment.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/weaviate/weaviate/main/deploy/k8s/autoscale.yaml

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/weaviate/weaviate/main/deploy/k8s/monitoring.yaml

```

> This command applies the autoscaling configuration.
> This command sets up monitoring for your Weaviate cluster.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Weaviate
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Weaviate cluster and all associated resources. Be aware that this will lead to data loss.

```bash

weaviate delete cluster --name weaviate-cluster --region us-central1

gcloud container clusters delete weaviate-cluster --region us-central1 --quiet

```

> This command deletes the cluster and all its resources.
> This command ensures all orphaned resources are cleaned up.
commands: gcloud container clusters delete weaviate-cluster --region us-central1 --quiet

upgrade.title: Upgrade Weaviate
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This process includes draining nodes if necessary, upgrading the cluster, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

weaviate upgrade cluster --version 1.36.2

kubectl get pods -n weaviate

```

> Replace <node-name> with the actual node name.
> This command upgrades your Weaviate cluster to the specified version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Weaviate'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if the Weaviate service is not running or the kubeconfig is not set up correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure the Weaviate service is running and check your kubeconfig with 'kubectl config view'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that your cluster does not have enough resources allocated for the Weaviate pods.

**Fix:**
Increase the node count or resource limits in your cluster configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Pod is in CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually happens due to misconfiguration or insufficient resources for the Weaviate pod.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unauthorized access'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur if RBAC is not configured correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the correct roles and bindings are set up for your user.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new vectorization models and improved RBAC features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old vectorization API endpoints.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.35.x, ensure to update your API calls to use the new endpoints.

resolution.summary: You should see output from 'kubectl get pods' showing all Weaviate pods in 'Running' status.
codeSnippets: curl -LO https://github.com/weaviate/weaviate/releases/download/v1.36.2/weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 && chmod +x weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 && sudo mv weaviate-cli-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/weaviate
gcloud container clusters get-credentials weaviate-cluster --region us-central1

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have the required tools and resources before proceeding with the installation.
